← the Atlas

kolu-tui — a CLI terminal client for kolu-server

design · seedling ·MAY 30, 2026

A terminal-side client of kolu-server's in-process pty-host — list / attach / spawn from the shell, no browser and no daemon. The CLI face of kolu and the seed of a tmux replacement.

Contents

  • Architecture — everything stays in-process in kolu-server
  • Why a second client at all
  • The surface — ~150 LOC in packages/pty-tui
    • Decisions recorded here (to be echoed in code comments)
  • Phasing — three beta increments, all in-process
    • Phase 0 — a minimal CI-runner TUI (the 3rd surface example) shipped
  • The whole user flow — CLI workflows + screens
    • A · Interactive session
    • B · Headless test — client-death detach/reattach, no browser
  • Later — how this dovetails with the daemon plan (out of scope)

remote-terminals › pty-daemon (R-4) › kolu-tui  ·  sibling: chrome-bar rail

A terminal-side client of kolu-server’s pty-host. kolu-server owns its PTYs in-process today; kolu-tui connects to that same in-process pty-host over a local unix socket (speaking ptyHostSurface) and gives you list / attach / spawn from the terminal — no browser, no daemon. It is the CLI face of kolu and the seed of a tmux/zmx replacement ( #671 ), shipped in phases as a beta. This doc is scoped to kolu-tui-on-the-in-process-server; the parent plan’s Phase B (moving the pty-host into a surviving daemon) is a separate plan it later dovetails with — see Later.

Architecture — everything stays in-process in kolu-server

No new process. kolu-server keeps owning the PTYs exactly as it does now; it just exposes the same in-process router over an additional local socket so a second client — the CLI — can reach it. The web path is byte-identical.

         ┌─────────────┐                          ┌──────────────────┐
       │   browser   │                          │     kolu-tui     │
       │  (the GUI)  │                          │ packages/pty-tui │
       └──────┬──────┘                          └─────────┬────────┘
              │  ws://  kolu-common/contract              │  ptyHostSurface
              │  rich: PTY + providers + sessions         │  raw: PTY + taps
              ▼                                           ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  kolu-server   —   ONE process · all in-process · NO daemon            │
│                                                                        │
│                    servePtyHost(deps).router                          │
│        (owns node-pty fds + @xterm/headless mirror + the VT taps)      │
│                   ╱                              ╲                      │
│            directLink                      serveOverStdio              │
│       (web path — unchanged)            (NEW — additive, the SAME      │
│              ╱                            router on a unix socket)     │
│             ▼                                       ▼                   │
│   LocalTerminalBackend                  ◆ $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/kolu/       │
│   (providers, sessions…)                  pty-host.sock ◆ ◀── kolu-tui │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The split by richness. The browser is the rich client — it speaks kolu-server’s public contract and gets the whole world (PTY plus the provider DAG: git context, agent state, PR status, session grouping). kolu-tui is the raw client — it speaks ptyHostSurface directly and gets the bare multiplexer (PTY plus the VT taps), exactly the tmux altitude. Both attach to the one in-process pty-host.

The only server change is one additive socket

servePtyHost(deps).router already exists and is already consumed in-process via directLink. Phase 1 adds serveOverStdio({ router }) on a unix socket beside it — the same router, a second transport. The in-process web path doesn’t change; spawning, killing, and metadata all still flow through LocalTerminalBackend as today. No PTY moves, no process is added, nothing survives a restart that didn’t before.

Why a second client at all

Two reasons, both standing on their own without any reference to daemons.

  • It’s a feature. A persistent-while-the-server-runs terminal you drive from the shell — the CLI face of kolu, the start of the tmux replacement #671 has always pointed at.
  • It proves the contract is consumer-agnostic. A1 ( #1055 ) asserts ptyHostSurface is a clean seam — kolu-server talks to the pty-host through a contract, not through node-pty internals. The only real proof of that is a second, independent consumer. If writing a terminal client against the surface is clean, the seam is at the right altitude (the “framework needs an end-to-end demo, not just unit tests” lesson — parent plan, lesson #3). If it’s awkward, that’s a finding worth having early.

Honest scope

Because the pty-host is in-process, restarting kolu-server (a deploy) kills its PTYs — kolu-tui does not make terminals survive a server restart. What it does give is client-side detach/reattach: the CLI can come and go (close the terminal, reopen, re-attach) while kolu-server keeps running and holds the PTY. Surviving a server restart is the separate daemon plan (B), not this.

The surface — ~150 LOC in packages/pty-tui

A small node CLI in its own package, packages/pty-tui, that dials the unix socket through stdioLink (the same link the daemon would later use) and gets a typed ptyHostSurface client. No new wire, no new framing — serveOverStdio is already unit-tested via the loopback fixture.

SubcommandSurface callBehaviour
kolu-tui listterminal.list()One-shot — print each live PTY’s id, pid, cwd, title. The pty-host’s inventory.
kolu-tui attach <id>terminalAttach.get({id}) (stream) + terminal.write / terminal.resizePut the local tty in raw mode. First yield is the scrollback snapshot → paint to stdout; deltas stream after. Forward stdin via write; SIGWINCH → resize({id, cols, rows}). A line-start ~. escape (see below) detaches — the CLI exits, kolu-server keeps the PTY.
kolu-tui spawn [-- cmd]terminal.spawn({id: uuid, …}) then attachMint a UUID client-side, spawn, immediately attach. --print-id prints the id and does not attach (for scripting).
kolu-tui snapshot <id>terminalAttach.get({id}) — first yield onlyOne-shot, non-interactive: dump the current scrollback + a trailer line (pid · cwd · title) and exit. The scriptable primitive the headless test asserts on.
kolu-tui kill <id>terminal.kill({id})Out-of-band termination — exercises the order-safe kill path (abort the exit tap then kill) from a non-browser client.

The taps (subscribeCwd / subscribeTitle / subscribeForeground / exitPromise) feed the status line — a second, non-browser consumer of the raw VT taps, proving those are transport-clean too. --json makes list machine-readable; --socket <path> points at a non-default server.

Contract reality check

The subcommand → surface mapping above is shorthand; these are the exact shapes the current ptyHostSurface exposes (verified against @kolu/pty-host after A1 #1055 and the link-family unification #1059 ). Carry these into the phases so the CLI is written against what the contract actually offers, not the sketch:

  • gap terminal.list returns { entries }, and entries have no title. The output is { entries: TerminalListEntry[] } (not a bare array), and each entry is { id, pid, cwd, lastActivity } — title lives only on the title tap. So Phase 1’s list table must either drop the TITLE column or source it from surface.title.get({id}) per row, and list --json / the headless test’s jq must read .entries[], not .[].
  • gap terminal.spawn has no command field — it always spawns $SHELL. The spawn input is { id?, cwd?, cols?, rows?, scrollback? }; there is no command/argv. So Phase 3’s spawn [-- cmd] (and the headless test’s spawn --print-id -- bash -c …) needs a deliberate decision: add a command field to the contract (a PTY_HOST_CONTRACT_VERSION bump) or write the command into the spawned shell. This is the one real contract gap behind the flow examples below.
  • The taps are host-method names; on the wire they’re streams. subscribeCwd/subscribeTitle/subscribeForeground/exitPromise are PtyHost methods below the seam — a CLI client consumes the surface streams surface.cwd.get({id}) / surface.title.get({id}) / surface.foreground.get({id}) / surface.exit.get({id}). Note exit crosses as a one-shot stream (yields { exitCode } once, then ends), not a Promise — the exit tap is awaited as the first frame of that stream.
  • The socket doesn’t exist yet — and the path convention needs a decision. No serveOverStdio listener exists in packages/server today (that is the Phase 1 work). But the doc’s $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/kolu/pty-host.sock diverges from the existing runtime-dir convention $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/kolu-<serverProcessId>/ (per-process, ephemeral — see koluRoot.ts). A discoverable socket arguably wants a stable kolu/ dir, but Phase 1 should choose that path deliberately (and define the fallback when XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is unset) rather than inherit the per-PID dir by accident.

What’s unchanged and confirmed: servePtyHost(deps).router, directLink (web path), serveOverStdio, stdioLink, and the terminalAttach.get({id}) snapshot-then-delta stream all exist with the names this doc uses — the seam A1 promised is intact, and the shipped mini-ci example (Phase 0) already drives the same stdioLink / serveOverStdio framing end-to-end.

Decisions recorded here (to be echoed in code comments)

DecisionChoiceWhy
Homea tiny separate packages/pty-tuiAn independent CLI package, not a bin folded into @kolu/pty-host — keeps the pty-host package focused on the contract + primitive, and keeps the CLI’s own deps (raw-tty handling, arg parsing) out of the closure the A2 staleKey hashes.
Render fidelityraw VT passthroughWrite the pty-host’s bytes straight to stdout; do not re-render through a second @xterm/headless mirror. Simplest and truest — and it avoids a second rendering path drifting from the server’s. This trade-off must be recorded as a design-decision comment at the passthrough site in code.
Detach / escapessh-style line-start ~ escape — never Ctrl+BA passthrough multiplexer must not steal any control char the inner tools need — Ctrl+B and Ctrl+J are reserved by Claude Code (input/prohibitedKeybinds.ts), and editors/shells claim the rest. So the escape is the unambiguous ssh model: a ~ recognised only immediately after a newline you typed. ~. detach · ~k kill · ~~ literal tilde · ~? help. The char is configurable (--escape); a literal ~ mid-line passes through untouched, so every inner-app chord, Ctrl+B included, reaches the program unmodified.

Phasing — three beta increments, all in-process

Phases 1–3 build kolu-tui on the in-process server — each leaves Kolu working, ships on its own, and changes nothing in the web path. They’re preceded by a Phase 0 that lives outside kolu entirely: a surface example that rehearses the whole “interactive TUI over oRPC stdio” pattern (detail below).

PhaseShipsServer changeUser-visible
0 · surface example shipped
(rehearsal, outside kolu)		A minimal CI-runner TUI over oRPC stdio in @kolu/surface’s examples — the 3rd example, after the worker demo + the remote-process-monitor (→ drishti). Ships the runner the drishti way via HostSession; the default pipeline runs real typecheck CI. shipped #1073 None — separate from kolu-server.A standalone example (could graduate like drishti); proves the pattern and becomes kolu-tui’s reference skeleton.
1 · list
(the socket + the round-trip)		kolu-server serves its in-process pty-host router over a unix socket (serveOverStdio); packages/pty-tui with list + read-only snapshot.+1 local socket listener on the existing router. Web path byte-identical.A beta CLI that lists your live terminals. + a “Terminal UI (beta)” line in the README/site.
2 · attach
(the interactive client)		Raw-tty passthrough, snapshot-then-delta, stdin→write, SIGWINCH→resize, the ~-escape detach.None beyond Phase 1.Drive a terminal from the CLI; detach and re-attach while the server runs.
3 · spawn / kill
(the raw multiplexer)		spawn (+ --print-id), kill, list --json.None.Create and kill PTYs from the CLI — a usable raw multiplexer (beta). The richer tmux-replacement UX (windows, panes, config) grows from here, as its own work.

Phase 1 alone is the smallest honest first ship of kolu-tui — exactly the kolu-tui list starting point: a one-shot RPC, no raw-tty mode, the “hello world” that proves serveOverStdio-over-a-real-socket + stdioLink + the contract round-trip, at near-zero risk.

Phase 0 — a minimal CI-runner TUI (the 3rd surface example) shipped #1073

Before touching kolu at all, prove the pattern in @kolu/surface’s examples — the falsifiability test of lesson #3 applied to “interactive TUI over oRPC stdio,” the way the worker demo and the remote-process-monitor (which became drishti) validated the earlier patterns. The candidate: a minimal CI runner — justci-flavoured but self-contained, deliberately not the real justci (no Haskell, no GitHub statuses, no multi-platform fan-out). Just a small DAG of shell commands — runnable locally, or on a single remote host where the prebuilt runner closure is shipped the drishti way (nix copy + realise over ssh, via @kolu/surface-nix-host’s HostSession; see below).

As built (#1073, merged)

Shipped as @kolu/surface-example-mini-ci. Two things landed differently from this section’s original sketch, both deliberately: the remote transport pivoted from git archive source-ship to the drishti HostSession closure-ship (so the example exercises the very R-2 transport the parent plan builds on, not a minimal cousin of it), and the default pipeline runs real typecheck CI for the remote-process-monitor example (a surface + nix-host → monitor diamond of tsc --noEmit gates) rather than a toy build → test → lint. The dashboard also binds plain keys (digits/n/p/r/q), not the ~-escape — it renders structured state and owns the keyboard, so it has no need for kolu-tui’s raw-passthrough escape (that decision is recorded for kolu-tui below). One constraint worth carrying forward: the runner ships as a read-only nix closure, so only read-only tasks (typecheck) belong in the default pipeline — write-heavy steps (vite build, nix build) would need a writable copy of the workspace first.

It’s a clean structural twin of kolu-tui: a long-lived runner owns the DAG + each node’s process + its log buffer, and streams to ephemeral TUI clients over stdio. The plan’s nodes.list() / node.log(id) / node.rerun(id) map onto the framework-idiomatic spelling the surface derives — a nodes cell (surface.nodes.get({})) ↔ kolu-tui’s list; a nodeLog stream (surface.nodeLog.get({id}), snapshot-then-delta) ↔ attach; a node.rerun procedure (surface.node.rerun({id})) ↔ input. That this was clean to write against the surface primitives is the finding: the seam is at the right altitude for kolu-tui to inherit. The example is now kolu-tui’s copy-paste skeleton (raw-tty setup, the snapshot-paint loop) and a permanent regression test for the pattern.

  ┌──────────────────────┐   oRPC over stdio · HostSession (stdioLink / serveOverStdio)   ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
│     mini-ci TUI      │ ── nodes.get · nodeLog.get(id) · node.rerun(id) ──────────────▶│  mini-ci-runner (via HostSession)  │
│ (the example client) │ ◀─ nodes cell + per-node nodeLog stream ───────────────────────│  DAG: surface, nix-host ─▶ monitor │
└──────────────────────┘                                                                └────────────────────────────────────┘
mini-ci · pipeline remote-process-monitor · attached: monitor● running

✔ surface (2.3s) ✔ nix-host (1.9s) ▶ monitor running… ──▶ attached (needs: surface, nix-host) ──────────────────────────────── $ pnpm —filter @kolu/surface-example-remote-process-monitor typecheck $ tsc —noEmit ❯ type-checking…

1-9 attachn/p cycler rerunq quit● 1 running · 2 ok · 0 pending

Same surface primitives kolu-tui needs — a node-state cell (snapshot+delta), a per-node log stream (attach-by-id), and input (rerun) — exercised end-to-end with a real, useful little tool that can graduate to its own repo.

Remote mode — the drishti HostSession closure-ship over ssh as-built

mini-ci runs the DAG locally by default, but it can also run on a remote host — and it ships the runner the drishti way, exactly as the remote-process-monitor example does: a prebuilt mini-ci-runner nix closure is shipped with nix copy to the host, realised with nix-store --realise, then run as ssh host mini-ci-runner --stdio, with the TUI attached over stdio-over-ssh. connect() reads the host-arch runner .drv from MINI_CI_RUNNER_DRV (required) and hands it to @kolu/surface-nix-host’s getHostSession({ host, binary, resolveDrvPath }), which owns the ref-count, reconnect, watchdog, and a copying → connecting → connected state cell. localhost skips the nix copy and runs the realised binary directly — the same HostSession, only the transport differs. So the example exercises the surface’s stdio link in both placements over the one HostSession — the same R-2 / drishti closure-ship transport the parent plan builds on. (This is the drishti transport itself, not a git archive cousin of it: a closure, not source — the trade-off being that only read-only tasks belong in the shipped closure.)

  ┌──────────────┐  oRPC over ssh stdio · HostSession ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  mini-ci TUI │ ── nodes.get · nodeLog.get(id) ───▶│   host : mini-ci-runner --stdio │
│   (local)    │ ◀─ nodes cell + nodeLog stream ────│   1. nix copy closure + realise │
└──────────────┘                                    │   2. run the DAG over ssh stdio │
                                                    └─────────────────────────────────┘

The whole user flow — CLI workflows + screens

The interactive loop a user walks, then the same loop scripted as a headless test. The dark screens are what kolu-tui paints. The running example: a PTY 3f9a…c21 in ~/code/kolu that becomes a Claude Code session.

A · Interactive session

0 · Is the server’s pty-host reachable? — honest failure, never a hang

$ kolu-tui list
kolu-tui: no pty-host socket at /run/user/1000/kolu/pty-host.sock (ECONNREFUSED)
          is kolu-server running? the socket appears once it boots.

An unreachable pty-host is a clear, immediate error — not a silent empty hang.

1 · Birth — spawn a shell and attach to it

$ kolu-tui spawn
spawned 3f9a…c21 (pid 12843, cwd ~/code/kolu) · attaching…
kolu-tui · attached 3f9a…c21 · zsh● live

(fresh PTY — empty scrollback) ~/code/kolu ❯ █

~. detach~k kill~? help● live · 102×31

2 · Work — the raw VT taps drive the status line live

As the foreground process and the title change, the subscribeForeground / subscribeTitle taps push updates and the status rail tracks them — no browser, no server-side providers involved.

kolu-tui · attached 3f9a…c21 · claude: implement pty-tui● live

~/code/kolu ❯ claude ⏺ Bash(git status) ⎿ On branch master — working tree clean > █

● livefg nodetitle ↻ "claude: implement pty-tui"102×31

3 · The inventory — list (from another shell)

kolu-tuistdioLink → /run/user/1000/kolu/pty-host.sock

$ kolu-tui list

ID ST PID CWD TITLE 3f9a…c21 ● 12843 ~/code/kolu claude: implement pty-tui 7b2e…0d4 ● 12901 ~/code/kolu/.worktrees/… zsh a18c…9ff ● 13044 ~/scratch vim notes.md

● 3 live PTYs (in kolu-server, in-process)pty-host pid 9981

4 · Detach — the server keeps the PTY

<Enter>~.
detached 3f9a…c21 · the pty-host (in kolu-server) keeps it running
$

The CLI exits; kolu-server is untouched and the PTY runs on. (This is client-side detach — distinct from server survival. If kolu-server itself restarts, the in-process PTY dies; that persistence is the separate daemon plan.)

5 · Re-attach — the scrollback snapshot repaints instantly

The first yield of the attach stream is the full scrollback snapshot; deltas follow. The CLI comes and goes; the running server holds the state.

kolu-tui · re-attached 3f9a…c21 · claude: implement pty-tui↻ reattached

↻ snapshot restored — 1,284 lines · PTY pid 12843 unchanged ⏺ Bash(git status) ⎿ On branch master — working tree clean > █

● live (reattached by id)server up throughout · PTY pid unchanged

6 · Exit — the exit tap fires, the client returns to the shell

> /exit                       # the agent (or ^D) ends the PTY
3f9a…c21 exited (code 0) · exit tap fired · detaching
$

Out of band, kolu-tui kill <id> drives the same order-safe teardown (abort the exit tap then kill) from the CLI.

B · Headless test — client-death detach/reattach, no browser

The detach/reattach loop scripted. It asserts that the CLI client can die and a fresh one re-joins the same PTY (the server runs throughout) — fast, no browser.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# 1 · spawn a PTY with a unique marker in its scrollback (no attach)
id=$(kolu-tui spawn --print-id -- bash -c 'echo "MARK-$$"; exec sleep 1d')
pid_before=$(kolu-tui list --json | jq -r ".[] | select(.id==\"$id\").pid")

# 2 · (no kolu-tui process is attached — simulating client death)

# 3 · a fresh client re-attaches by id and asserts the state is intact
kolu-tui snapshot "$id" | grep -q "MARK-"            # scrollback preserved
pid_after=$(kolu-tui list --json | jq -r ".[] | select(.id==\"$id\").pid")
[ "$pid_before" = "$pid_after" ]                     # same PTY, not a respawn

echo "✓ client came and went; server held the PTY (pid $pid_after unchanged)"

This is the kolu-tui-scoped reattach test — the client can come and go while the server holds the PTY. (Surviving a server restart is a different assertion that belongs to the daemon plan, not here.)

Later — how this dovetails with the daemon plan (out of scope)

Stated only so we don’t trip over it: when the parent plan’s Phase B eventually moves the pty-host out of kolu-server into a surviving kolu --stdio daemon, kolu-tui’s socket target shifts from kolu-server to the daemon — with no contract change, because both serve the same ptyHostSurface. At that point the CLI’s terminals gain server-restart survival for free, and the raw-vs-rich client split is unchanged. But that is a separate plan; nothing in kolu-tui’s phases above depends on it, and kolu-tui ships and is useful entirely on the in-process server.

Child of pty-daemon · sibling of chrome-bar.

← the Atlas github ↗
